ISEN 620 Syllabus

Section 700 (14035)

Survey of Optimization

Fall 2025 - College Station

Course Information

Meeting Times:

Meeting Type: LEC

Meeting Days: Asynchronous

Start Time: N/A

End Time: N/A

Start Date: 08/25/2025

End Date: 12/16/2025

Meeting Location:  ONLINE

Credit Hours:  3

Instructor Details

Sergiy Butenko

Email:  butenko@tamu.edu

Office:  ETB 4037

Phone:  979-458-2333

Office Hours

TR 10:00-11:00 AM or by appointment

Course Description

This is an introductory course discussing theory and numerical methods for

deterministic linear and nonlinear optimization. Topics to be discussed include

linear programming, unconstrained and constrained nonlinear optimization,

including classical optimality conditions and numerical algorithms.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  MATH 304 or MATH 311.

Special Course Designation

None

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Analyze  and  apply  linear and nonlinear programming theory and algorithms.

Formulate  linear and nonlinear optimization models from real-world problems.

Solve  small-scale optimization problems by hand and  utilize  optimization software to

solve large-scale problems.

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Required

Course Handbook

URL for Resource:

https://drive.google.com/file/d/1KOF0V8vKpe1CiqwJAJ24

9Ubqrsqv-IXw/view?usp=sharing

This material Is: Recommended

Numerical Methods and Optimization

ISBN:  9781466577787

Authors:  Sergiy Butenko, Panos M. Pardalos

Publisher:  CRC Press

Publication Date:  2014-03-11

Notes:

The course manual with all the materials required for

successful completion of the course is provided. In

addition, it is recommended (but not required) to have

at least one reference text covering linear and nonlinear

optimization, such as Numerical Methods and

Optimization: An Introduction by Butenko and Pardalos,

ISBN: 978-1466577770 or Linear and Nonlinear

Optimization by Griva, Nash and Sofer, ISBN 978-0-

898716-61-0. Additional references: Introduction to

Mathematical Programming by Winston and

Venkataramanan, Fourth Edition, Duxbury; Linear

Programming and Network Flows by Bazarra, Jarvis and

optimization, such as Numerical Methods and

Optimization: An Introduction by Butenko and Pardalos,

ISBN: 978-1466577770 or Linear and Nonlinear

Optimization by Griva, Nash and Sofer, ISBN 978-0-

898716-61-0. Additional references: Introduction to

Mathematical Programming by Winston and

Venkataramanan, Fourth Edition, Duxbury; Linear

Programming and Network Flows by Bazarra, Jarvis and

Sherali, Second Edition, Wiley; An Introduction to

Optimization by Chong and Zak, Second Edition, Wiley.

Additional Instructional Materials

This Material is: Required

Software and Computing Resources

Notes:

The course is computing-intensive. The required advanced optimization software

(AMPL) will be made available for download on Canvas (for Windows, Mac, and

Linux). You must ensure access to a computer that runs AMPL and Excel (or a

compatible spreadsheet application with Solver add-on).

Grading Policy

All graded assignments will be open book/notes and must be completed via Canvas.

It is crucial that all the graded work is completed on your own, in accordance with

the Aggie Honor Code and the rules specified for each assignment. Any violations of

academic integrity rules will result in severe penalties, such as F ∗  for the course and

report to the honor council. A preliminary, automatically generated grade will

appear on Canvas immediately upon submission of your work, however, this grade

may not be final, as some assignments will require submitting additional materials

(such as a scan of your work and/or computer codes) for grading.

There will be the total of 40 graded assignments, described next.

•  4 Exams – 20% each. The exams will be open book/notes. Each exam will be

proctored to maintain scholastic integrity of the program. The instructor will provide

Zoom proctoring. If the exam dates/times specified in the detailed schedule below

do not work for you, you should notify the instructor no later than Friday preceding

the first exam, so that alternative arrangements can be made.

•  11 Homework Quizzes – 15%. The homework quizzes will be conducted via

Canvas, no proctor is required. The quizzes will be based on the practice problems,

solutions to which are provided in the course handbook (“Solution Manual”

chapter). The quizzes will be timed, but you will have the flexibility to take them any

time before the midnight of the quiz date specified in the course schedule.

• 25 Lecture Quizzes – 5%. Lecture quizzes are based on the videos of lectures and

should be taken according to the schedule at page 6.

solutions to which are provided in the course handbook (“Solution Manual”

chapter). The quizzes will be timed, but you will have the flexibility to take them any

time before the midnight of the quiz date specified in the course schedule.

• 25 Lecture Quizzes – 5%. Lecture quizzes are based on the videos of lectures and

should be taken according to the schedule at page 6.

The final letter grades will be calculated using the formula

weighted average = 0.05(lec quiz average)+0.15(hw quiz average)+0.8(exam

average)

and will be assigned as follows. A: [90,100], B: [80,90), C: [70,80), D: [60,70), F: [0,60).

Optional Final Exam will be offered as a chance to improve your final grade. The

exam will consist of the problems of the same types as the four exams given during

the semester. You will be able to choose which problems to solve. You will have two

hours to complete the exam, and the score for each problem you attempt will

replace the score you obtained for the problem of the same type in the original

exam (whenever the new score is higher).

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

While you are expected to follow the schedule above for best learning outcomes,

the due dates are not strictly enforced. Late submissions will not be penalized, as

long as they are completed before the exam covering the corresponding material. If

an exam is missed, you must contact the instructor as soon as possible with a

written authorized excuse. Make up exams will be given in accordance with

University Rules (see Rule 7 at  http://student-rules.tamu.edu ).

Additional Course Information

Course Format and Expectations

Section 700: The distance learning version of the course does not involve regular

class meetings, however, individual or group meetings with the instructor can be

arranged by appointment. You are expected to complete graded assignments

(lecture quizzes, homework quizzes, and exams) according to the schedule outlined

in “Course Topics and Schedule” section of this document (page 6). Each chapter of

the course handbook covers one lecture, with the material provided in the form of

text and pointers to pre-recorded videos of lectures available on YouTube and

Google Drive.

Expected Time Commitment

The course is structured in a way that requires timely attention to the material in

in “Course Topics and Schedule” section of this document (page 6). Each chapter of

the course handbook covers one lecture, with the material provided in the form of

text and pointers to pre-recorded videos of lectures available on YouTube and

Google Drive.

Expected Time Commitment

The course is structured in a way that requires timely attention to the material in

order to succeed. You are expected to allocate a similar amount of time to the

course as you would with a regular 3-credit on-campus course. More specifically,

you are expected to spend around 4.5 hours 1  per week attending the lectures or

reviewing the videos of lectures (the actual video files are shorter, taking into

account the time for activities, such as taking notes, during which you would pause

the video). In addition to the lecture time, you are expected to spend at least 9

hours per week on homework assignments, such as reading the course manual,

solving problems, and implementing and running optimization models. You should

be prepared to dedicate sufficient time in order to master the material at the level

that will benefit your professional career.

Communication

The following means of communication will be used during the course: Canvas,

GroupMe, email, Zoom. The course website will be maintained through Canvas. This

site will contain all the required course materials, including the lecture notes, slides,

software packages used, homework assignments, quizzes, exams, and grades.

Please check the web site daily – you are expected to be aware of any information

posted on it within 24 hours. You will need to use your TAMU NetId to log in. In

addition, the course-related discussions will be facilitated using a GroupMe chat.

You are strongly encouraged to join the course group (the link and the QR code are

provided above). Communication via GroupMe provides the quickest way of getting

your questions concerning the course answered.

All major announcements concerning the course will be communicated to the class

via email (in addition to announcements on Canvas and GroupMe). Zoom will be

used for proctoring the exams as well as office hours. You will be required to log in

Zoom with your Net ID and use video for exams. The exam sessions will be

recorded.

1 An academic hour is 50 minutes.
